title: Array values should not be replaced unconditionally
description: Array values should not be replaced unconditionallyAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearreliabilityBugsuspiciousWhy is this an issue?Storing a value inside a collection at a given key or index and then unconditionally overwriting it without reading the initial value is a case of a "dead store".towns[i] = "London"; towns[i] = "Chicago"; // Noncompliant: We never used the previous valueThis practice is redundant and will cause confusion for the reader. More importantly, it is often an error and not what the developer intended to do.Available In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Cloud, GitLab repositoriesAnalyze code in youron-premise CIDeveloper EditionAvailable Since9.1©2025SonarSource Sàrl. All rights reserved.Privacy Policy|C
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
towns[i] = "London";
towns[i] = "Chicago";  // Noncompliant: We never used the previous value

data[0] = 10;
data[0] = 20;          // Noncompliant: First assignment is wasted

buffer[idx] = 'A';
buffer[idx] = 'B';    // Noncompliant: Previous character never read

values[j] = 1.5;
values[j] = 2.7;      // Noncompliant: Dead store of first value
compliant:
buffer[idx] = 'A';
if (buffer[idx] == 'A') buffer[idx] = 'B';  // Compliant: Value checked first

values[j] = getValue();
values[k] = 2.7;  // Compliant: Different index variables

checks.message: Array value replaced unconditionally. The previous assignment is a dead store that may indicate a bug or redundant code.
